text: The video shows the case of a second stage procedures namely a laparoscopic completion proctectomy and J-pouch anal anastomosis in a seventy-two years old patient diagnosed with ulcerative colitis. The patient underwent laparoscopic total colectomy with end ileostomy for steroid-dependent refractory disease three months before.

The video displays the placement of five trocars for the laparoscopic procedure. The camera port is positioned above the umbilicus, while two operating trocars are located in the lower and upper right quadrants. Two additional trocars are placed in the left flank near the pubic region to assist with rectal retraction.

The surgeon begins the operation with a peristomal incision to mobilize the terminal ileostomy, which is then closed using a linear stapler. An Alexis retractor is placed at the previous ileostomy site, facilitating the creation of pneumoperitoneum. The apex of the pouch is demonstrated and marked on the abdominal skin.
Inside the abdomen, the surgeon performs a uteropexy to enhance pelvic exposure. The rectal stump is identified and stitched to facilitate effective rectal traction and counter traction.
The surgeon begins a nerve-sparing rectal dissection from the posterior plane, identifying the avascular plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the mesorectal fascia.

The surgeon continues the dissection down to the pelvic floor, exposing the puborectalis muscle. The dissection then shifts to lateral and anterior mobilization, with careful rectal dissection to avoid injury to autonomic pelvic nerves. The anterior dissection is visibly developed behind the rectovaginal septum along the rectal muscularis propria.
The surgeon uses a 45mm linear stapler with a double cartridge to staple the rectum approximately two centimeters above the dentine line after fully skeletonizing the anorectal junction.

The surgeon positions themselves between the patient's legs to fully mobilize the small bowel mesentery up to its origin at the duodenum. The small bowel is exteriorized, and intracorporeal ileal mobilization results in a visible gain in length of three centimeters.
The surgeon removes mesenteric fat between the ileocolic vessels and the terminal branches of the superior mesenteric vessels, gaining an additional centimeter in length of the small bowel.
The surgeon performs a mesenteric serosal incision perpendicular to the mesenteric axis on both sides of the mesentery, as part of the third elongation technique.

The surgeon demonstrates a five-centimeter elongation at the apex of the pouch achieved through various maneuvers. If further elongation is needed, the terminal part of the superior mesenteric vessels is ligated after testing the blood supply with a vascular clamp, allowing an additional three-centimeter gain, as shown in a patient with short mesentery.

The surgeon fashions an ileal J pouch using multiple fires of a linear stapler, creating a thin pouch approximately one centimeter long. After reinduction of the pneumoperitoneum, an ileal pouch anal anastomosis is performed using a 29mm circular stapler. The procedure concludes with the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy and placement of a pelvic drain.
The patient was able to tolerate oral diet on first postoperative day. Hospital stay was uneventful and the patient was discharged on fifth postoperative day.
